book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Athanasius and Paul going to Rome, and having obtained Letters from Bishop Julius, recover their respective Dioceses.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Athanasius and Paul going to Rome, and having obtained Letters from Bishop Julius, recover their respective Dioceses.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Athanasius, meanwhile, after a lengthened journey, at last reached Italy.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The western division of the empire was then under the sole power of Constans, the youngest of Constantine’s sons, his brother Constantine having been slain by the soldiers, as was before stated.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At the same time also Paul, bishop of Constantinople, Asclepas of Gaza, Marcellus of Ancyra, a city of the Lesser Galatia, and Lucius of Adrianople, having been accused on various charges, and expelled from their several churches arrived at the imperial city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: There each laid his case before Julius, bishop of Rome.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He on his part, by virtue of the Church of Rome’s peculiar privilege, sent them back again into the East, fortifying them with commendatory letters; and at the same time restored to each his own place, and sharply rebuked those by whom they had been deposed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Relying on the signature of the bishop Julius, the bishops departed from Rome, and again took possession of their own churches, forwarding the letters to the parties to whom they were addressed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These persons considering themselves treated with indignity by the reproaches of Julius, called a council at Antioch, assembled themselves and dictated a reply to his letters as the expression of the unanimous feeling of the whole Synod.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It was not his province, they said, to take cognizance of their decisions in reference to any whom they might wish to expel from their churches; seeing that they had not opposed themselves to him, when Novatus was ejected from the church.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These things the bishops of the Eastern church communicated to Julius, bishop of Rome.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But, as on the entry of Athanasius into Alexandria, a tumult was raised by the partisans of George the Arian, in consequence of which, it is affirmed, many persons were killed; and since the Arians endeavor to throw the whole odium of this transaction on Athanasius as the author of it, it behooves us to make a few remarks on the subject.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: God the Judge of all only knows the true causes of these disorders; but no one of any experience can be ignorant of the fact, that such fatal accidents are for the most part concomitants of the factious movements of the populace.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It is vain, therefore, for the calumniators of Athanasius to attribute the blame to him; and especially Sabinus, bishop of the Macedonian heresy.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For had the latter reflected on the number and magnitude of the wrongs which Athanasius, in conjunction with the rest who hold the doctrine of consubstantiality, had suffered from the Arians, or on the many complaints made of these things by the Synods convened on account of Athanasius, or in short on what that arch-heretic Macedonius himself has done throughout all the churches, he would either have been wholly silent, or if constrained to speak, would have spoken more plausible words, instead of these reproaches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But as it is intentionally overlooking all these things, he willfully misrepresents the facts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He makes, however, no mention whatever of the heresiarch, desiring by all means to conceal the daring enormities of which he knew him to be guilty.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And what is still more extraordinary, he has not said one word to the disadvantage of the Arians, although he was far from entertaining their sentiments.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The ordination of Macedonius, whose heretical views he had adopted, he has also passed over in silence; for had he mentioned it, he must necessarily have recorded his impieties also, which were most distinctly manifested on that occasion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Let this suffice on this subject.
